question: What is $${1 \over {\sin 10^\circ }} - {{\sqrt 3 } \over {\cos 10^\circ }}$$ equal to?

en.content: What is $${1 \over {\sin 10^\circ }} - {{\sqrt 3 } \over {\cos 10^\circ }}$$ equal to?
en.explanation:
$${1 \over {\sin 10^\circ }} - {{\sqrt 3 } \over {\cos 10^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{\cos 10^\circ - \sqrt 3 \sin 10^\circ } \over {\sin 10^\circ \cos 10^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{2\left( {{1 \over 2}\cos 10^\circ - {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}\sin 10^\circ } \right)} \over {{1 \over 2} \times 2\sin 10^\circ \cos 10^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{2(\sin 30^\circ \cos 10^\circ - \cos 30^\circ \sin 10^\circ )} \over {{1 \over 2} \times \sin 20^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{4.\sin (30^\circ - 10^\circ )} \over {\sin 20^\circ }} = {{4\sin 20^\circ } \over {\sin 20^\circ }} = 4$$

question:
Consider the following for triangle ABC

1. $$\sin \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \cos \left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

2. $$\tan \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \cot \left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

3. $$\sin (B + C) = \cos A$$

4. $$\tan (B + C) = - \cot A$$

Which of the above are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following for triangle ABC

1. $$\sin \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \cos \left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

2. $$\tan \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \cot \left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

3. $$\sin (B + C) = \cos A$$

4. $$\tan (B + C) = - \cot A$$

Which of the above are correct?
en.explanation:
In $$\Delta$$ABC, we have,

$$\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{B + C} \over 2} = {{180^\circ } \over 2} - {A \over 2} = 90^\circ - {A \over 2}$$

1. $$\sin \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \sin \left( {90^\circ - {A \over 2}} \right) = \cos {A \over 2}$$

So, 1 is correct.

2. $$\tan \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \tan \left( {90^\circ - {A \over 2}} \right) = \cot (A/2)$$

So, 2 is correct.

3. $$B + C = 180^\circ - A^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (B + C) = \sin (180^\circ - A)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (B + C) = \sin A$$

So, 3 is not correct.

4. $$B + C = 180^\circ - A^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan (B + C) = \tan (180^\circ - A)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan (B + C) = - \tan A$$

So, 4 is not correct.

solution:
In $$\Delta$$ABC, we have,

$$\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{B + C} \over 2} = {{180^\circ } \over 2} - {A \over 2} = 90^\circ - {A \over 2}$$

1. $$\sin \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \sin \left( {90^\circ - {A \over 2}} \right) = \cos {A \over 2}$$

So, 1 is correct.

2. $$\tan \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right) = \tan \left( {90^\circ - {A \over 2}} \right) = \cot (A/2)$$

So, 2 is correct.

3. $$B + C = 180^\circ - A^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (B + C) = \sin (180^\circ - A)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (B + C) = \sin A$$

So, 3 is not correct.

4. $$B + C = 180^\circ - A^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan (B + C) = \tan (180^\circ - A)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan (B + C) = - \tan A$$

So, 4 is not correct.

title: NDA 2017 Paper 1

question: The expression $${{\sin \alpha + \sin \beta } \over {\cos \alpha + \cos \beta }}$$ is equal to

en.content: The expression $${{\sin \alpha  + \sin \beta } \over {\cos \alpha  + \cos \beta }}$$ is equal to
en.explanation:
$${{\sin \alpha + \sin \beta } \over {\cos \alpha + \cos \beta }}$$

$$ = \tan \left( {{{\alpha + \beta } \over 2}} \right)$$

question: What is the maximum value of the function f(x) = 4sin2 x + 1 ?

en.content: What is the maximum value of the function f(x) = 4sin<sup>2</sup> x + 1 ?
en.explanation:
Given,

f(x) = 4sin2 x + 1

We know,

0 $$\le$$ sin2 x $$\le$$ 1

$$\therefore$$ 0 $$\le$$ 4sin2 $$\le$$ 4

1 $$\le$$ 4sin2 x + 1 $$\le$$ 4 + 1

$$\therefore$$ Maximum value of f(x) = 5

question: If $$K = \sin \left( {{\pi \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{5\pi } \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{7\pi } \over {18}}} \right)$$, then what is the value of K?

en.content: If $$K = \sin \left( {{\pi  \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{5\pi } \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{7\pi } \over {18}}} \right)$$, then what is the value of K?
en.explanation:
$$K = \sin \left( {{\pi \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{5\pi } \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{7\pi } \over {18}}} \right)$$

$$ = \sin \left( {{{180^\circ } \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{5 \times 180^\circ } \over {18}}} \right)\sin \left( {{{7 \times 180^\circ } \over {18}}} \right)$$

[$$\because$$ $${\pi ^c} = 180^\circ $$]

$$ = \sin 10^\circ .\sin 50^\circ .\sin 70^\circ $$

$$ = \sin 10^\circ .\sin 50^\circ .cos20^\circ $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}[(2sin10^\circ \sin 50^\circ )].\cos 20^\circ $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}[\cos (10^\circ - 50^\circ ) - \cos (10^\circ + 50^\circ )].\cos 20^\circ $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\left[ {\cos 40^\circ - {1 \over 2}} \right].\cos 20^\circ $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\left[ {{1 \over 2}(2\cos 40^\circ \cos 20^\circ ) - {1 \over 2}\cos 20^\circ } \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\left[ {{1 \over 2}\{ \cos (40^\circ + 20^\circ ) + cos(40^\circ - 20^\circ )\} - {1 \over 2}\cos 20^\circ } \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\left[ {{1 \over 2}\{ \cos 60^\circ + \cos 20^\circ \} - {1 \over 2}\cos 20^\circ } \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\left[ {{1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 2}\cos 20^\circ - {1 \over 2}\cos 20^\circ } \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$

question: If $$\tan (\alpha + \beta ) = 2$$ and $$\tan (\alpha - \beta ) = 1$$, then tan(2$$\alpha$$) is equal to

en.content: If $$\tan (\alpha  + \beta ) = 2$$ and $$\tan (\alpha  - \beta ) = 1$$, then tan(2$$\alpha$$) is equal to
en.explanation:
$$\tan (2\alpha ) = \tan (\alpha + \beta + \alpha - \beta )$$

$$ = {{\tan (\alpha + \beta ) + \tan (\alpha - \beta )} \over {1 - \tan (\alpha + \beta ).\tan (\alpha - \beta )}}$$

$$ = {{2 + 1} \over {1 - 2 \times 1}} = {3 \over { - 1}} = - 3$$

question:
Given that tan$$\alpha$$ and tan$$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation x2 + bx + c = 0 with b $$\ne$$ 0.
What is tan ($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$) equal to?

en.direction: Given that tan$$\alpha$$ and tan$$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> + bx + c = 0 with b $$\ne$$ 0.
en.content: What is tan ($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$) equal to?
en.explanation:
Given, $${x^2} + bx + c = 0$$, $$b \ne 0$$

$$\tan \alpha + \tan \beta = - b$$

and $$\tan \alpha \tan \beta = c$$

Now, $$\tan (\alpha + \beta ) = {{\tan \alpha + \tan \beta } \over {1 - \tan \alpha \tan \beta }}$$

$$ = {{ - b} \over {1 - c}} = b{(c - 1)^{ - 1}}$$

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Trigonometric Angles and Equations • Mathematics
en.excerpt: What is tan ($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$) equal to?

solution:
Given, $${x^2} + bx + c = 0$$, $$b \ne 0$$

$$\tan \alpha + \tan \beta = - b$$

and $$\tan \alpha \tan \beta = c$$

Now, $$\tan (\alpha + \beta ) = {{\tan \alpha + \tan \beta } \over {1 - \tan \alpha \tan \beta }}$$

$$ = {{ - b} \over {1 - c}} = b{(c - 1)^{ - 1}}$$

question:
Consider a $$\Delta$$ABC in which $$\cos A + \cos B + \cos C = \sqrt 3 \sin {\pi \over 3}$$
What is the value of $$\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2}$$ ?

en.direction: Consider a $$\Delta$$ABC in which $$\cos A + \cos B + \cos C = \sqrt 3 \sin {\pi  \over 3}$$
en.content: What is the value of $$\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2}$$ ?
en.explanation:
Given, in $$\Delta$$ ABC,

$$\cos A + \cos B + \cos C = \sqrt 3 \sin {\pi \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos A + \cos B + \cos C = \sqrt 3 \times {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2} = {3 \over 2}$$

We know that,

$$\cos A + \cos B + \cos C = 1 + 4\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + 4\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2} = {3 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2} = {3 \over 2} - 1 = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$

question:
Consider the equation $$k\sin x + \cos 2x = 2k - 7$$
If the equation possesses solution, then what is the minimum value of k?

en.content: If the equation possesses solution, then what is the minimum value of k?
en.explanation:
Given equation is

$$k\sin x + \cos 2x = 2k - 7$$

$$ \Rightarrow k\sin x + 1 - 2{\sin ^2}x = 2k - 7$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 2{\sin ^2}x + k\sin x + 8 - 2k = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\sin ^2}x - k\sin x + 2k - 8 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\sin x = {{k \pm \sqrt {{k^2} - 16k + 64} } \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{k \pm \sqrt {{{(k - 8)}^2}} } \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{k \pm (k - 8)} \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{2k - 8} \over 4}$$ and sin x = 2

As, $$ - 1 \le \sin x \le 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 1 \le {{2k - 8} \over 4} \le 1 \Rightarrow - 4 \le 2k - 8 \le 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4 \le 2k \le 12 \Rightarrow 2 \le k \le 6$$

$$\therefore$$ Minimum value of k = 2

and maximum value of k = 6

The minimum value of k i s2.

question:
Consider the function $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$
What is the minimum value of the function f($$\theta$$) ?

en.content: What is the minimum value of the function f($$\theta$$) ?
en.explanation:
$$\because$$ $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

Also, $$1 - 2si{n^2}\theta = \cos 2\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}\theta = {{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2}$$ ..... (i)

and $$2{\cos ^2}\theta - 1 = \cos 2\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\theta = {{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^4}\theta = {\left( {{{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}} \right)^2}$$ ....(ii)

$$\therefore$$ $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2} + {{\left( {{{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2} + {{1 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta + 2\cos 2\theta } \over 4}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{2 - 2\cos 2\theta + 1 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta + 2\cos 2\theta } \over 4}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{3 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta } \over 4}} \right\} = 3 + {\cos ^2}2\theta $$

$$f{(\theta )_{\min }} = 3 + 0 = 3$$

question:
Consider the function $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$
Consider the following statements

1. $$f(\theta ) = 2$$ has no solution.

2. $$f(\theta ) = {7 \over 2}$$ has a solution.

Which of the above statement(s) is/are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following statements

1. $$f(\theta ) = 2$$ has no solution.

2. $$f(\theta ) = {7 \over 2}$$ has a solution.

Which of the above statement(s) is/are correct?
en.explanation:
$$\because$$ $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

Also, $$1 - 2si{n^2}\theta = \cos 2\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}\theta = {{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2}$$ ..... (i)

and $$2{\cos ^2}\theta - 1 = \cos 2\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\theta = {{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^4}\theta = {\left( {{{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}} \right)^2}$$ ....(ii)

$$\therefore$$ $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2} + {{\left( {{{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2} + {{1 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta + 2\cos 2\theta } \over 4}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{2 - 2\cos 2\theta + 1 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta + 2\cos 2\theta } \over 4}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{3 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta } \over 4}} \right\} = 3 + {\cos ^2}2\theta $$

Given, $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

$$ = 4[{\sin ^2}\theta + {({\cos ^2}\theta )^2}]$$

$$ = 4[si{n^2}\theta + {(1 - {\sin ^2}\theta )^2}]$$

$$ = 4[{\sin ^2}\theta + 1 + {\sin ^4}\theta - 2{\sin ^2}\theta ]$$

$$ = 4[{\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + 1]$$

1. When f($$\theta$$) = 2

$$ \Rightarrow 4({\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + 1) = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + 1 = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + {1 \over 2} = \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\sin ^4}\theta - 2{\sin ^2}\theta + 1 = 0$$

Let $${\sin ^2}\theta = x$$, then above equation becomes $$2{x^2} - 2x + 1 = 0$$

Now, $${b^2} - 4ac = {( - 2)^2} - 4 \times 2 \times 1$$

= 4 $$-$$ 8 = $$-$$ 4 < 0

$$\Rightarrow$$ f($$\theta$$) = 2 has no solution.

2. When $$f(\theta ) = {7 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4({\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + 1) = {7 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + 1 = {7 \over 8}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^4}\theta - {\sin ^2}\theta + {1 \over 8} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 8{\sin ^4}\theta - 8{\sin ^2}\theta + 1 = 0$$

Let $${\sin ^2}\theta = x$$, then $$8{x^2} - 8x + 1 = 0$$

Now, $${b^2} - 4ac = {( - 8)^2} - 4 \times 8 \times 1 = 64 - 32 = 32 > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {{ - ( - 8) \pm \sqrt {32} } \over {2 \times 8}}$$$$ = {{8 \pm 4\sqrt 2 } \over {16}} = {{2 \pm \sqrt 2 } \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}\theta = {{2 \pm \sqrt 2 } \over 4} \Rightarrow \sin \theta = {{\sqrt {2 \pm \sqrt 2 } } \over 2}$$

Using $$-$$ve sign,

$$\sin \theta = {{\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } } \over 2} = \sin 22{1 \over 2}^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = 22{1 \over 2}^\circ $$

Hence, $$f(\theta ) = {7 \over 2}$$ has a solution.

using +ve sign, $$\sin \theta = {{\sqrt {2 + \sqrt 2 } } \over 2} < 1$$, so it gives a solution.

question:
Consider a $$\Delta$$ABC in which $$\cos A + \cos B + \cos C = \sqrt 3 \sin {\pi \over 3}$$
What is the value of $$\cos \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{C + A} \over 2}} \right)$$ ?

en.direction: Consider a $$\Delta$$ABC in which $$\cos A + \cos B + \cos C = \sqrt 3 \sin {\pi  \over 3}$$
en.content: What is the value of $$\cos \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{C + A} \over 2}} \right)$$ ?
en.explanation:
$$\cos \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{C + A} \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = \cos \left( {90^\circ - {C \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {90^\circ - {A \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {90^\circ - {B \over 2}} \right)$$

[$$\because$$ $$A + B + C = 180^\circ $$]

$$ = \sin {C \over 2}\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}$$

$$ = \sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$ [from Q. No. 65]

solution:
$$\cos \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{B + C} \over 2}} \right)\cos \left( {{{C + A} \over 2}} \right)$$

[$$\because$$ $$A + B + C = 180^\circ $$]

$$ = \sin {C \over 2}\sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}$$

$$ = \sin {A \over 2}\sin {B \over 2}\sin {C \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$ [from Q. No. 65]

question:
Consider the following statements

1. If ABC is an equilateral triangle, then $$3\tan (A + B)\tan C = 1$$.

2. If ABC is a triangle in which A = 78$$^\circ$$, B = 66$$^\circ$$, then $$\tan \left( {{A \over 2} + C} \right) < \tan A$$

3. If ABC is any triangle, then $$\tan \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\sin \left( {{C \over 2}} \right) < \cos \left( {{C \over 2}} \right)$$

Which of the above statements is/are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following statements

1. If ABC is an equilateral triangle, then $$3\tan (A + B)\tan C = 1$$.

2. If ABC is a triangle in which A = 78$$^\circ$$, B = 66$$^\circ$$, then $$\tan \left( {{A \over 2} + C} \right) < \tan A$$

3. If ABC is any triangle, then $$\tan \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\sin \left( {{C \over 2}} \right) < \cos \left( {{C \over 2}} \right)$$

Which of the above statements is/are correct?
en.explanation:
1. Since, ABC is an equilateral triangle.

$$\therefore$$ $$\angle A = \angle B = \angle C = 60^\circ $$

Now, consider

$$3\tan (A + B)\tan C = 3\tan 120^\circ \tan 60^\circ $$

$$ = 3\tan (180^\circ - 60^\circ )\tan 60^\circ $$

$$ = ( - 3)\tan 60^\circ \tan 60^\circ $$

$$ = ( - 3).\sqrt 3 .\sqrt 3 = - 9$$

2. Consider,

$$\tan \left( {{A \over 2} + C} \right) = \tan \left( {{{78^\circ } \over 2} + 36^\circ } \right)$$ [$$\because$$ $$\angle C = 180^\circ - \angle A - \angle B = 180^\circ - 78^\circ - 66^\circ = 36^\circ $$]

$$ = \tan 75^\circ < \tan 78^\circ $$ [$$\because$$ value of tan$$\theta$$ increases, as $$\theta$$ varying from 0$$^\circ$$ to 90$$^\circ$$] < tan A

Hence, $$\tan \left( {{A \over 2} + C} \right) < \tan A$$

3. Consider,

$$\tan \left( {{{A + B} \over 2}} \right)\sin {C \over 2}$$

$$ = \tan \left( {{{180^\circ - C} \over 2}} \right)\sin {C \over 2}$$

$$ = \tan \left( {90^\circ - {C \over 2}} \right)\sin {C \over 2}$$

$$ = \cot {C \over 2}\sin {C \over 2}$$

$$ = {{\cos {C \over 2}} \over {\sin {C \over 2}}}.\sin {C \over 2}$$

$$ = \cos {C \over 2} > \cos {C \over 2}$$

question: If $$A = (\cos 12^\circ - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ + \sin 24^\circ )$$ and $$B = (\sin 60^\circ - \sin 12^\circ )(\cos 48^\circ - \cos 72^\circ )$$, then what is $${A \over B}$$ equal to?

en.content: If $$A = (\cos 12^\circ  - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ  + \sin 24^\circ )$$ and $$B = (\sin 60^\circ  - \sin 12^\circ )(\cos 48^\circ  - \cos 72^\circ )$$, then what is $${A \over B}$$ equal to?
en.explanation:
We have, $$A = (\cos 12^\circ - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ + \sin 24^\circ )$$

and $$B = (\sin 60^\circ - \sin 12^\circ )(\cos 48^\circ - \cos 72^\circ )$$

Now, consider

$$A = (\cos 12^\circ - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ + \sin 24^\circ )$$

$$ = - 2\sin 24^\circ \sin ( - 12^\circ )(2\sin 60^\circ \cos 36^\circ )$$

$$ = 4\sin 24^\circ \sin 12^\circ \sin 60^\circ \cos 36^\circ $$

and $$B = 2\cos 36^\circ \sin 24^\circ ( - 2\sin 60^\circ \sin ( - 12^\circ ))$$

$$ = 4\sin 24^\circ \sin 12^\circ \sin 60^\circ \cos 36^\circ $$

Clearly, $${A \over B} = 1$$

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Trigonometric Angles and Equations • Mathematics
en.excerpt: If $$A = (\cos 12^\circ  - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ  + \sin 24^\circ )$$ and $$B = (\sin 60^\circ  - \sin 12^\circ )(\cos 48^\circ  - \cos 72^\circ )$$, then what is $${A \over B}$$ equal to?

solution:
We have, $$A = (\cos 12^\circ - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ + \sin 24^\circ )$$

and $$B = (\sin 60^\circ - \sin 12^\circ )(\cos 48^\circ - \cos 72^\circ )$$

Now, consider

$$A = (\cos 12^\circ - \cos 36^\circ )(\sin 96^\circ + \sin 24^\circ )$$

$$ = - 2\sin 24^\circ \sin ( - 12^\circ )(2\sin 60^\circ \cos 36^\circ )$$

$$ = 4\sin 24^\circ \sin 12^\circ \sin 60^\circ \cos 36^\circ $$

and $$B = 2\cos 36^\circ \sin 24^\circ ( - 2\sin 60^\circ \sin ( - 12^\circ ))$$

$$ = 4\sin 24^\circ \sin 12^\circ \sin 60^\circ \cos 36^\circ $$

Clearly, $${A \over B} = 1$$

question:
Consider the function $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$
What is the maximum value of the function $$f(\theta )$$ ?

en.content: What is the maximum value of the function $$f(\theta )$$ ?
en.explanation:
$$\because$$ $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

Also, $$1 - 2si{n^2}\theta = \cos 2\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}\theta = {{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2}$$ ..... (i)

and $$2{\cos ^2}\theta - 1 = \cos 2\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\theta = {{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^4}\theta = {\left( {{{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}} \right)^2}$$ ....(ii)

$$\therefore$$ $$f(\theta ) = 4({\sin ^2}\theta + {\cos ^4}\theta )$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2} + {{\left( {{{1 + \cos 2\theta } \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{1 - \cos 2\theta } \over 2} + {{1 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta + 2\cos 2\theta } \over 4}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{2 - 2\cos 2\theta + 1 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta + 2\cos 2\theta } \over 4}} \right\}$$

$$ = 4\left\{ {{{3 + {{\cos }^2}2\theta } \over 4}} \right\} = 3 + {\cos ^2}2\theta $$

$$\therefore$$ $$f{(\theta )_{\max }} = 3 + 1 = 4$$

question:
Consider the equation $$k\sin x + \cos 2x = 2k - 7$$
If the equation possesses solution, then what is the maximum value of k?

en.content: If the equation possesses solution, then what is the maximum value of k?
en.explanation:
Given equation is

$$k\sin x + \cos 2x = 2k - 7$$

$$ \Rightarrow k\sin x + 1 - 2{\sin ^2}x = 2k - 7$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 2{\sin ^2}x + k\sin x + 8 - 2k = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\sin ^2}x - k\sin x + 2k - 8 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\sin x = {{k \pm \sqrt {{k^2} - 16k + 64} } \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{k \pm \sqrt {{{(k - 8)}^2}} } \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{k \pm (k - 8)} \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{2k - 8} \over 4}$$ and sin x = 2

As, $$ - 1 \le \sin x \le 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 1 \le {{2k - 8} \over 4} \le 1 \Rightarrow - 4 \le 2k - 8 \le 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4 \le 2k \le 12 \Rightarrow 2 \le k \le 6$$

$$\therefore$$ Minimum value of k = 2

and maximum value of k = 6

The maximum value of k is 6.

question:
Given that tan$$\alpha$$ and tan$$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation x2 + bx + c = 0 with b $$\ne$$ 0.
What is $$\sin (\alpha + \beta )sec\alpha sec\beta $$ equal to?

en.direction: Given that tan$$\alpha$$ and tan$$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> + bx + c = 0 with b $$\ne$$ 0.
en.content: What is $$\sin (\alpha  + \beta )sec\alpha sec\beta $$ equal to?
en.explanation:
$$\because$$ We know that,

$$\tan \alpha + \tan \beta = - b$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\sin \alpha } \over {\cos \alpha }} + {{\sin \beta } \over {\cos \beta }} = - b$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\sin \alpha \cos \beta + \cos \alpha \sin \beta } \over {\cos \alpha \cos \beta }} = - b$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\sin (\alpha + \beta )} \over {\cos \alpha \cos \beta }} = - b$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (\alpha + \beta )sec\alpha sec\beta = - b$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (\alpha + \beta )sec\alpha sec\beta = - b$$

question: If $$x\cos \theta + y\sin \theta = z$$, then what is the value of $${(x\sin \theta - y\cos \theta )^2}$$ ?

en.content: If $$x\cos \theta  + y\sin \theta  = z$$, then what is the value of $${(x\sin \theta  - y\cos \theta )^2}$$ ?
en.explanation:
We have,

$$x\cos \theta + y\sin \theta = z$$

On squaring both sides, we get

$${x^2}{\cos ^2}\theta + {y^2}{\sin ^2}\theta + 2xy\sin \theta \cos \theta = {z^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2}(1 - {\sin ^2}\theta ) + {y^2}(1 - {\cos ^2}\theta ) + 2xy\sin \theta \cos \theta = {z^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - {z^2} = {x^2}{\sin ^2}\theta + {y^2}{\cos ^2}\theta - 2xy\sin \theta \cos \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - {z^2} = {(x\sin \theta - y\cos \theta )^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${(x\sin \theta - y\cos \theta )^2} = {x^2} + {y^2} - {z^2}$$

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 2
en.subTitle: Trigonometric Angles and Equations • Mathematics

solution:
We have,

$$x\cos \theta + y\sin \theta = z$$

On squaring both sides, we get

$${x^2}{\cos ^2}\theta + {y^2}{\sin ^2}\theta + 2xy\sin \theta \cos \theta = {z^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2}(1 - {\sin ^2}\theta ) + {y^2}(1 - {\cos ^2}\theta ) + 2xy\sin \theta \cos \theta = {z^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - {z^2} = {x^2}{\sin ^2}\theta + {y^2}{\cos ^2}\theta - 2xy\sin \theta \cos \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - {z^2} = {(x\sin \theta - y\cos \theta )^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${(x\sin \theta - y\cos \theta )^2} = {x^2} + {y^2} - {z^2}$$

question: What is the value of $$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{5\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$ ?

en.content: What is the value of $$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi  \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{5\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$ ?
en.explanation:
$$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{5\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left\{ {1 + \cos \left( {\pi - {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)} \right\}\left\{ {1 + \cos \left( {\pi - {\pi \over 8}} \right)} \right\}$$

$$ = \left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 - \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 - \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {1 - {{\cos }^2}{\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 - {{\cos }^2}{{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$

$$ = {\sin ^2}{\pi \over 8}{\sin ^2}{{3\pi } \over 8} = {\left( {\sin {\pi \over 8}\sin {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {\left\{ {\sin {\pi \over 8}\sin \left( {{\pi \over 2} - {\pi \over 8}} \right)} \right\}^2} = {\left( {\sin {\pi \over 8}\cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 4}{\left( {\sin {\pi \over 4}} \right)^2} = {1 \over 4} \times {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 2
en.subTitle: Trigonometric Angles and Equations • Mathematics
en.excerpt: What is the value of $$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi  \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{5\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$ ?

solution:
$$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{5\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left\{ {1 + \cos \left( {\pi - {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)} \right\}\left\{ {1 + \cos \left( {\pi - {\pi \over 8}} \right)} \right\}$$

$$ = \left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 - \cos {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 - \cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {1 - {{\cos }^2}{\pi \over 8}} \right)\left( {1 - {{\cos }^2}{{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)$$

$$ = {\sin ^2}{\pi \over 8}{\sin ^2}{{3\pi } \over 8} = {\left( {\sin {\pi \over 8}\sin {{3\pi } \over 8}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {\left\{ {\sin {\pi \over 8}\sin \left( {{\pi \over 2} - {\pi \over 8}} \right)} \right\}^2} = {\left( {\sin {\pi \over 8}\cos {\pi \over 8}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 4}{\left( {\sin {\pi \over 4}} \right)^2} = {1 \over 4} \times {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$

question: What is $${{1 - \tan 2^\circ \cot 62^\circ } \over {\tan 152^\circ - \cot 88^\circ }}$$ equal to?

en.content: What is $${{1 - \tan 2^\circ \cot 62^\circ } \over {\tan 152^\circ  - \cot 88^\circ }}$$ equal to?
en.explanation:
We have,

$${{1 - \tan 2^\circ \cot 62^\circ } \over {\tan 152^\circ - \cot 88^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{1 - \tan (90^\circ - 88^\circ )\cot 62^\circ } \over {\tan (180^\circ - 28^\circ ) - \cot 88^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{1 - \cot 88^\circ \cot 62^\circ } \over { - \tan 28^\circ - \cot 88^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{1 - \cot 88^\circ \cot 62^\circ } \over { - \tan (90^\circ - 62^\circ ) - \cot 88^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{1 - \cot 88^\circ \cot 62^\circ } \over { - \cot 62^\circ - \cot 88^\circ }} = {{\cot 62^\circ \cot 88^\circ - 1} \over {\cot 62^\circ + \cot 88^\circ }}$$

$$ = \cot (62^\circ + 88^\circ )$$

$$ = \cot 150^\circ = - \sqrt 3 $$

question:
$$\sin A + 2\sin 2A + \sin 3A$$ is equal to which of the following?

I. $$4\sin 2A{\cos ^2}\left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

II. $$2\sin 2A{\left( {\sin {A \over 2} + \cos {A \over 2}} \right)^2}$$

III. $$8\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}\left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

Select the correct answer using the code given below.

en.content:
$$\sin A + 2\sin 2A + \sin 3A$$ is equal to which of the following?

I. $$4\sin 2A{\cos ^2}\left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

II. $$2\sin 2A{\left( {\sin {A \over 2} + \cos {A \over 2}} \right)^2}$$

III. $$8\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}\left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
en.explanation:
We have,

$$\sin A + 2\sin 2A + \sin 3A$$

$$ = \sin A + \sin 3A + 2\sin 2A$$

$$ = 2\sin 2A\cos A + 2\sin 2A$$

$$\because$$ $$\left[ {\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin {{A + B} \over 2}\cos {{A - B} \over 2}} \right]$$

$$ = 2\sin 2A(1 + \cos A)$$

$$ = 2\sin 2A.2co{s^2}{A \over 2}$$

$$ = 4\sin 2A{\cos ^2}{A \over 2}$$ [ part I ]

$$ = 4.2\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}{A \over 2}$$

$$ = 8\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}{A \over 2}$$ [ part III ]

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 2
en.subTitle: Trigonometric Angles and Equations • Mathematics
en.excerpt: $$\sin A + 2\sin 2A + \sin 3A$$ is equal to which of the following?I. $$4\sin 2A{\cos ^2}\left( {{A \over 2}} \right)$$II. $$2\sin 2A{\left( {\sin {A \over 2} + \cos {A \over 2}} \right)^2}$$III. $$8\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}\left( {{A \over 2}}...

solution:
We have,

$$\sin A + 2\sin 2A + \sin 3A$$

$$ = \sin A + \sin 3A + 2\sin 2A$$

$$ = 2\sin 2A\cos A + 2\sin 2A$$

$$\because$$ $$\left[ {\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin {{A + B} \over 2}\cos {{A - B} \over 2}} \right]$$

$$ = 2\sin 2A(1 + \cos A)$$

$$ = 2\sin 2A.2co{s^2}{A \over 2}$$

$$ = 4\sin 2A{\cos ^2}{A \over 2}$$ [ part I ]

$$ = 4.2\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}{A \over 2}$$

$$ = 8\sin A\cos A{\cos ^2}{A \over 2}$$ [ part III ]

question: If $$\sin {\theta _1} + \sin {\theta _2} + \sin {\theta _3} + \sin {\theta _4} = 4$$, then what is the value of $$\cos {\theta _1} + \cos {\theta _2} + \cos {\theta _3} + \cos {\theta _4}$$ ?

en.content: If $$\sin {\theta _1} + \sin {\theta _2} + \sin {\theta _3} + \sin {\theta _4} = 4$$, then what is the value of $$\cos {\theta _1} + \cos {\theta _2} + \cos {\theta _3} + \cos {\theta _4}$$ ?
en.explanation:
We have,

$$\sin {\theta _1} + \sin {\theta _2} + \sin {\theta _3} + \sin {\theta _4} = 4$$

Since, maximum value of sin$$\theta$$ = 1

$$\therefore$$ $$\sin {\theta _1} = \sin {\theta _2} = \sin {\theta _3} = \sin {\theta _4} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\theta _1} = {\theta _2} = {\theta _3} = {\theta _4} = 90^\circ $$

$$\therefore$$ $$\cos {\theta _1} + \cos {\theta _2} + \cos {\theta _3} + \cos {\theta _4} = 0$$ [$$\because$$ $$\cos 90^\circ = 0$$]

solution:

$$\therefore$$ $$\cos {\theta _1} + \cos {\theta _2} + \cos {\theta _3} + \cos {\theta _4} = 0$$ [$$\because$$ $$\cos 90^\circ = 0$$]

question: If $$x = \sin 70^\circ \sin 50^\circ $$ and $$y = \cos 60^\circ \cos 80^\circ $$, then what is xy equal to?

en.content: If $$x = \sin 70^\circ \sin 50^\circ $$ and $$y = \cos 60^\circ \cos 80^\circ $$, then what is xy equal to?
en.explanation:
We have,

$$x = \sin 70^\circ \sin 50^\circ $$

$$y = \cos 60^\circ \cos 80^\circ $$

$$\therefore$$ $$xy = \sin 70^\circ \sin 50^\circ \cos 60^\circ \cos 80^\circ $$

$$ = \cos 20^\circ \cos 40^\circ \cos 60^\circ \cos 80^\circ $$

[$$\because$$ $$\sin (90^\circ - \theta ) = \cos \theta $$]

$$ = \cos 60^\circ \cos 20^\circ \cos (60^\circ - 20^\circ )\cos (60^\circ + 20^\circ )$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times {{\cos 3 \times 20^\circ } \over 4}$$

[$$\because$$ $$\cos x\cos (60^\circ - x)\cos (60^\circ + x) = {{\cos 3x} \over 4}$$]

$$ = {1 \over 8}\cos 60^\circ = {1 \over 8} \times {1 \over 2} = {1 \over {16}}$$

[$$\because$$ $$\sin (90^\circ - \theta ) = \cos \theta $$]

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times {{\cos 3 \times 20^\circ } \over 4}$$

[$$\because$$ $$\cos x\cos (60^\circ - x)\cos (60^\circ + x) = {{\cos 3x} \over 4}$$]

$$ = {1 \over 8}\cos 60^\circ = {1 \over 8} \times {1 \over 2} = {1 \over {16}}$$

question: If $$\sin 18^\circ = {{\sqrt 5 - 1} \over 4}$$, then what is the value of sin81$$^\circ$$ ?

en.content: If $$\sin 18^\circ  = {{\sqrt 5  - 1} \over 4}$$, then what is the value of sin81$$^\circ$$ ?
en.explanation:
Given,

$$\sin 18^\circ = {{\sqrt 5 - 1} \over 4}$$

Now,

$${(\sin 18^\circ )^2} = 1 - {\cos ^2}18^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{{\sqrt 5 - 1} \over 4}} \right)^2} = 1 - {\cos ^2}18^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{5 + 1 - 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}} = 1 - {\cos ^2}18^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{6 - 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}} = 1 - {\cos ^2}18^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}18^\circ = 1 - \left( {{{6 - 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}18^\circ = {{10 + 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos 18^\circ = {{\sqrt {10 + 2\sqrt 5 } } \over 4}$$

Since, $$\cos {\theta \over 2} = \sqrt {{{1 + \cos \theta } \over 2}} $$ [$$\because$$ $$\theta = 18^\circ $$]

$$\therefore$$ $$\cos 9^\circ = \sqrt {{{{{1 + \sqrt {10 + 2\sqrt 5 } } \over 4}} \over 2}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos (90^\circ - 81^\circ ) = \sqrt {{{8 + 2\sqrt {10 + 2\sqrt 5 } } \over {16}}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin 81^\circ = \sqrt {{{8 + 2\sqrt {10 + 2\sqrt 5 } } \over {16}}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{{3 + \sqrt 5 + 5 - \sqrt 5 + 2\sqrt {3 + \sqrt 5 } .\sqrt {5 - \sqrt 5 } } \over {16}}} $$

$$ = {{\sqrt {(3 + \sqrt 5 } + \sqrt {5 - \sqrt 5 } } \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{6 - 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}} = 1 - {\cos ^2}18^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}18^\circ = 1 - \left( {{{6 - 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}18^\circ = {{10 + 2\sqrt 5 } \over {16}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos 18^\circ = {{\sqrt {10 + 2\sqrt 5 } } \over 4}$$

Since, $$\cos {\theta \over 2} = \sqrt {{{1 + \cos \theta } \over 2}} $$ [$$\because$$ $$\theta = 18^\circ $$]

$$\therefore$$ $$\cos 9^\circ = \sqrt {{{{{1 + \sqrt {10 + 2\sqrt 5 } } \over 4}} \over 2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{{3 + \sqrt 5 + 5 - \sqrt 5 + 2\sqrt {3 + \sqrt 5 } .\sqrt {5 - \sqrt 5 } } \over {16}}} $$

$$ = {{\sqrt {(3 + \sqrt 5 } + \sqrt {5 - \sqrt 5 } } \over 4}$$

question:
If $$p = \tan \left( { - {{11\pi } \over 6}} \right)$$, $$q = \tan \left( {{{21\pi } \over 4}} \right)$$ and $$r = \cot \left( {{{283\pi } \over 6}} \right)$$, then which of the following is/are correct?

1. The value of p $$\times$$ r is 2.

2. p, q and r are in GP.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.

en.content:
If $$p = \tan \left( { - {{11\pi } \over 6}} \right)$$, $$q = \tan \left( {{{21\pi } \over 4}} \right)$$ and $$r = \cot \left( {{{283\pi } \over 6}} \right)$$, then which of the following is/are correct?

1. The value of p $$\times$$ r is 2.

2. p, q and r are in GP.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
en.explanation:
$$p = \tan \left( { - {{11\pi } \over 6}} \right) = - \tan {{11\pi } \over 6}$$

$$ = - \tan \left( {2\pi - {\pi \over 6}} \right)$$

$$ = \tan {\pi \over 6} = {1 \over {\sqrt 3 }}$$

$$q = \tan {{21\pi } \over 4} = \tan \left( {5\pi + {\pi \over 4}} \right)$$

$$ = \tan {\pi \over 4} = 1$$

$$r = \cot \left( {{{283\pi } \over 6}} \right) = \cot \left( {47\pi + {\pi \over 6}} \right)$$

$$ = \cot {\pi \over 6} = \sqrt 3 $$

1. $$p \times r = {1 \over {\sqrt 3 }} \times \sqrt 3 = 1$$ which is incorrect.

2. p, q and r are in GP.

$$ = \tan {\pi \over 4} = 1$$

$$r = \cot \left( {{{283\pi } \over 6}} \right) = \cot \left( {47\pi + {\pi \over 6}} \right)$$

question: On simplifying $${{{{\sin }^3}A + \sin 3A} \over {\sin A}} + {{{{\cos }^3}A - \cos 3A} \over {\cos A}}$$ we get

en.content: On simplifying $${{{{\sin }^3}A + \sin 3A} \over {\sin A}} + {{{{\cos }^3}A - \cos 3A} \over {\cos A}}$$ we get
en.explanation:
$${{{{\sin }^3}A + \sin 3A} \over {\sin A}} + {{{{\cos }^3}A - \cos 3A} \over {\cos A}}$$

$$ = {{{{\sin }^3}A + (3\sin A - 4{{\sin }^3}A)} \over {\sin A}} + {{{{\cos }^3}A - (4{{\cos }^3}A - 3\cos A)} \over {\cos A}}$$

[$$\because$$ $$\sin 3\theta = 3\sin \theta - 4{\sin ^3}\theta ,\cos 3\theta = 4{\cos ^3}\theta - 3\cos \theta $$]

$$ = ( - 3{\sin ^2}A + 3) + ( - 3{\cos ^2}A + 3)$$

$$ = 6 - 3({\sin ^2}A + {\cos ^2}A) = 6 - 3(1) = 3$$

$$ = {{{{\sin }^3}A + (3\sin A - 4{{\sin }^3}A)} \over {\sin A}} + {{{{\cos }^3}A - (4{{\cos }^3}A - 3\cos A)} \over {\cos A}}$$

[$$\because$$ $$\sin 3\theta = 3\sin \theta - 4{\sin ^3}\theta ,\cos 3\theta = 4{\cos ^3}\theta - 3\cos \theta $$]

$$ = 6 - 3({\sin ^2}A + {\cos ^2}A) = 6 - 3(1) = 3$$

question: If $$\sin x + \sin y = a$$ and $$\cos x + \cos y = b$$, then $${\tan ^2}\left( {{{x + y} \over 2}} \right) + {\tan ^2}\left( {{{x - y} \over 2}} \right)$$ is equal to

en.content: If $$\sin x + \sin y = a$$ and $$\cos x + \cos y = b$$, then $${\tan ^2}\left( {{{x + y} \over 2}} \right) + {\tan ^2}\left( {{{x - y} \over 2}} \right)$$ is equal to
en.explanation:
We have,

$$\sin x + \sin y = a$$ .....(i)

$$\cos x + \cos y = b$$ .....(ii)

On squaring and adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$1 + 1 + 2(\sin x.\sin y + \cos x.\cos y) = {a^2} + {b^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2 + 2\cos (x - y) = {a^2} + {b^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos (x - y) = {{{a^2} + {b^2} - 2} \over 2}$$ ..... (iii)

Now, on dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get

$${{\sin x + \sin y} \over {\cos x + \cos y}} = {a \over b}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan {{x + y} \over 2} = {a \over b}$$ ......(iv)

Now, $${\tan ^2}\left( {{{x + y} \over 2}} \right) + {\tan ^2}\left( {{{x - y} \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}} + {{1 - \cos (x - y)} \over {1 + \cos (x - y)}}$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}} + {{1 - {{{a^2} + {b^2} - 2} \over 2}} \over {1 + {{{a^2} + {b^2} - 2} \over 2}}}$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}} + {{4 - {a^2} - {b^2}} \over {{a^2} + {b^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{a^4} + {a^2}{b^2} + 4{b^2} - {a^2}{b^2} - {b^4}} \over {{a^2}{b^2} + {b^4}}}$$

$$ = {{{a^4} - {b^4} + 4{b^2}} \over {{a^2}{b^2} + {b^4}}}$$

en.title: NDA 2015 Paper 2
en.subTitle: Trigonometric Angles and Equations • Mathematics

solution:
We have,

$$\sin x + \sin y = a$$ .....(i)

$$\cos x + \cos y = b$$ .....(ii)

On squaring and adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$1 + 1 + 2(\sin x.\sin y + \cos x.\cos y) = {a^2} + {b^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2 + 2\cos (x - y) = {a^2} + {b^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos (x - y) = {{{a^2} + {b^2} - 2} \over 2}$$ ..... (iii)

Now, on dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get

$${{\sin x + \sin y} \over {\cos x + \cos y}} = {a \over b}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan {{x + y} \over 2} = {a \over b}$$ ......(iv)

Now, $${\tan ^2}\left( {{{x + y} \over 2}} \right) + {\tan ^2}\left( {{{x - y} \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}} + {{1 - \cos (x - y)} \over {1 + \cos (x - y)}}$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}} + {{1 - {{{a^2} + {b^2} - 2} \over 2}} \over {1 + {{{a^2} + {b^2} - 2} \over 2}}}$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}} + {{4 - {a^2} - {b^2}} \over {{a^2} + {b^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{a^4} + {a^2}{b^2} + 4{b^2} - {a^2}{b^2} - {b^4}} \over {{a^2}{b^2} + {b^4}}}$$

$$ = {{{a^4} - {b^4} + 4{b^2}} \over {{a^2}{b^2} + {b^4}}}$$
